topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink bled across the bottom of the paper, a stark, unforgiving slash against the pale expanse of the page. Sarah stared at the 'D-'. It felt like a physical weight pressing down on her chest, making each breath shallow and tight. Her hands, usually steady enough to ace any exam, were trembling slightly as she traced the circled score with a fingernail. The fluorescent lights of the library hummed with an irritating, high-pitched drone, amplifying the silence in her head where coherent thoughts used to reside. She reread the feedback, the professor's notes a dense thicket of critique that seemed to spiral out of control. A dull ache bloomed behind her eyes, and the urge to simply crumple the paper and throw it into the nearest bin was almost irresistible. She pushed her chair back, the scrape of metal on linoleum sounding deafeningly loud.

[story 2]

"A C-minus?" Mark whispered, the sound barely audible above the clatter of keyboards in the study room. He leaned back in his chair, the cheap plastic groaning under his weight. He picked at a loose thread on his sweater, his gaze fixed on the wall calendar, which seemed to mock him with its relentless march toward deadlines. Every equation, every essay outline, felt tangled and knotted, a Gordian knot he simply couldn't untangle. He rubbed his temples, the pressure throbbing in his skull. He took a long, ragged breath, the air catching painfully in his throat. He looked at the syllabus, the dense text swimming before his eyes, and felt a sudden, paralyzing inertia. He just wanted the noise to stop, the expectations to dissolve into nothingness.

[story 3]

I looked at the grade as if it were a foreign language. It wasn't just a number; it was a verdict, a judgment delivered with cold, clinical precision. My jaw tightened until my teeth ached. I started pacing the small confines of my dorm room, the worn carpet muffling my hurried footsteps. Each step seemed to propel me further into a fog, a thick, gray substance that made focusing on anything impossible. I stopped by the window, staring out at the rain-slicked campus, the world outside seeming impossibly clear and organized compared to the internal chaos churning within me. I pressed my palms against the cool glass, trying to anchor myself, but my fingers felt clumsy, unresponsive. The silence in the room was heavy, expectant, waiting for me to shatter.
